intent: ADVERSARIAL forward-compat: the canonical read denial with extra UNKNOWN k=v fields appended after permissive= (newfield=foo trailing2=42), as a newer kernel might add. f4 section 1.1: only the four guaranteed SELinux fields are parsed positionally; everything else is optional k=v and unknown trailers must be ignored, not rejected.
_kinds_note: authoritative_category in {TeAllowable, Constraint, Bounds} (libsepol/audit2why replay); floor_label in {none, Permissive, MlsSuspected, RoleSuspected} (record-only heuristic). The trailing unknown fields do not affect either layer - the four SELinux fields are intact, so this is a plain TeAllowable read.

oracle.audit2allow_N: allow logrotate_t shadow_t:file read;  (audit2allow silently ignores newfield=/trailing2= and emits the normal single-perm allow)
oracle.audit2allow_R: auth_read_shadow(logrotate_t)  (the broad macro form; trailers ignored)
oracle.narrower_than_R_by: omits the auth_read_shadow() macro (typeattribute can_read_shadow_passwords + 5-perm read_file_perms on shadow_t + 6-perm list_dir_perms on the unrelated etc_t). expected.te emits only the single denied `read` perm.

triage_expectation.suspected_trap: A parser with a rigid grammar that expects permissive= to be the LAST field (anchored end-of-line regex, or a fixed field-count) would fail on a record that appends newfield=foo trailing2=42. The robust behavior is to parse the four guaranteed fields wherever they appear and ignore unknown trailing k=v - so the parser survives the first kernel that adds a field. audit2allow already tolerates this.
